paragraphs.body: I remember being driven to practice and dreading running because if our coach caught somebody cutting the corner he told us we had to run another lap. Our coach of 5 years for travel basketball had this rule: when we did our warmup laps, if he saw anyone cut the corner, he would make the entire team run another lap. This would also apply when we ran suicides, start at one base line run to the foul line then back then to half-court then back then the foul line of the opposite side of the court then back then one final time run to the other baseline back. It would also apply when running seventeens, which was side line to side line seventeen times, or when running anything. If he saw just one person not touching the line or cutting the corner he would make everyone redo the lap. When running he would always say “You cut corners here you cut corners in life”, whenever he wanted to to remind us of the consequences if we decided to cut the corner. This phrase that my coach had said those years ago has stuck with me for all of these years now. “Two roads diverged in a yellow wood, And sorry I could not travel both And be one traveler, long I stood And looked down one as far as I could To where it bent in the undergrowth;” (Asad) For years I would hear this phrase being said to us or screamed at us depending on what kind of mood our coach was in. And when I was younger I never quite understood why he constantly would say it when we all knew that there was a consequence to be had if we cut the corner or did not touch the line. We all knew from a young age that something bad was going to happen if he caught you trying to cheat your way through the laps. We all knew that if one person did it then we all would be punished for it. We all knew the consequences of our actions and knew that it would affect everyone on the team. But why did some girls still decide to do it even though it would affect everyone else on the team? It is questions like this as to why I think I still remember this quote all these years later.

annotations.text: “Two roads diverged in a yellow wood, And sorry I could not travel both And be one traveler, long I stood And looked down one as far as I could To where it bent in the undergrowth;”

paragraphs.body: Travel basketball started when I was in third grade and ended in eighth grade. I grew up with those girls and would spend countless hours practicing a sport that we all played. Some of those girls I also played softball with during that same time and for years after basketball ended. We all jokingly say that hearing this phrase years after basketball ended brings up some trauma and causes some uncomfortable feelings. But for me, I think now I can see the real meaning behind the phrase that sort of became a catchphrase of our coach after all those years. I believe that our coach was trying to tell us that in life you cannot always cut your way through life. I believe that the meaning behind the phrase was teaching us something about how the world works and how your actions might affect you and the people around you. “Then took the other, as just as fair, And having perhaps the better claim, Because it was grassy and wanted wear; Though as for that the passing there Had worn them really about the same,”(Asad) I have also seen this through working in retail and dealing with customers and their specific requests sometimes. When working in retail we have always been told that the customer comes first and sometimes told that the customers are always right. When dealing with a tricky customer you can never try and shorten the interaction. You can never blow over their many request, ideas, and wants. You cannot stand there and tell them that they are wrong when they think they are right. Certain customers will need you to hold their hands throughout their entire shopping process even if it includes telling them how much something is even though it is marketed. Dealing with these customers I have learned that you cannot cut corners without a consequence coming up on the other side of the road. Just like in basketball when cutting corners causes everyone to run more laps, trying to minimize your time with a picky customer will result in a bad reputation for the brand or store.

paragraphs.body: In life, you might think that taking a shortcut will cause you to be happier and cause you to get something done faster but in reality it will only cause pain. The truth of the matter is that when you try and do something faster than it should be done it will sometimes succeed but most of the time it will backfire. It resulted in more laps and more running when getting caught. When trying to deal with a picky customer and half fasting it caused a bad reputation for the store. I have learned through watching people do it and other life experiences you can never just half-fast anything. If you try and half-fast anything then you will always deal with some unpleasant consequence. In school, if you half-fast on an assignment and just take somebody else’s answers without learning the topic. Then the test comes around and you realize that you do not know anything that is going to be on the test so you decide to pull an all-nighter the night before the exam. Then you take your exam and get your results and you fail the test that would be the consequence of trying to half-fast something or trying to “cut corners in life”. I think having heard this quote from such a young age and having it stick with me for years now has caused me to think about every action that I have taken apart of. I think that it has caused me to never cut the corners in life or else something nasty is going to happen to me. I think that it has caused me to think about what I do more carefully than I would have if I had not heard this quote growing up. I believe that the quote really started to mean something to me when I entered high school and started to work. I could see this saying in action and how it impacts people of all ages and walks of life. Through school, I have seen what happens when you try and get away with something that you know will get you in trouble if you get caught. And through work, I have seen what it will do for the overall store if someone tries to not give a customer their full attention.

annotations.text: In life, you might think that taking a shortcut will cause you to be happier and cause you to get something done faster but in reality it will only cause pain.

paragraphs.body: I think I have learned a very valuable lesson from this phrase that I heard constantly growing up and have grown as a person from it. If I did not know of this quote I could possibly not understand the value of doing everything to your full extent or not know the consequences of my actions. I know that throughout your educational years, you constantly hear about if you get caught what will happen to you but that is if you get caught and that could take time. While at practice the consequence occurred right after you got caught you got punished. It taught me that in life you will always receive some sort of consequence if you get caught doing something deviant or not acceptable. It has taught me to always be honest and respectful in any situation.

annotations.text: It has taught me to always be honest and respectful in any situation.

paragraphs.body: “I shall be telling this with a sigh Somewhere ages and ages hence: Two roads diverged in a wood, and I— I took the one less traveled by, And that has made all the difference.” (Asad)

paragraphs.body: In the end, running the laps and the suicides were never the worst thing about practices they were just the things that could cause something else to happen. They were just the things that people would always try to half-fast which was never a good idea. They were the things that taught me the most about life and what happens when you try to shorthand life. They were the things that even though killed me sometimes caused me to think about what was at stack for everyone. To tell the truth, they were the things that I still think about to this day because their meaning was not to build up stamina. Their meaning was to never try and take the shortcut in life or else something unpleasant might happen that you did not want to happen. Work Cited Asad, Omer. “Summary and Analysis of the Road Not Taken.” Poem Analysis, 26 Nov. 2015, poemanalysis.com/robert-frost/the-road-not-taken/.
